EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Earl moved north of Puerto Rico early in the month. Although the storm never made landfall, it was responsible for shifting the local wind flow from the south. The islands were under this moist flow for around a week. Adding local effects to the mix, active afternoons were observed, with strong showers and thunderstorms. Several flood and mudslide reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported a mudslide in Road PR-167, indicating multiple individuals trapped in a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Fiona made landfall on Sunday the 18th around 3:20 PM AST near Punta Toc��n in southwestern Puerto Rico.  Strong, gusty winds accompanied much of the stronger convection embedded within the hurricane. Hurricane-force gusts were observed over portions of southern Puerto Rico during the afternoon on Sunday. These gusty winds, combined with ground saturation, resulted in damage across the island due to fallen trees and power lines. ||Fiona resulted in excessive rainfall, especially for southern into eastern Puerto Rico.  In areas along the Cordillera and around El Yunque, widespread rainfall totals of over 16 inches were seen, most of which fell on Sunday. Preliminary estimates suggest that over 24 inches of rain were seen locally, mostly around Cayey and southern Caguas and Aibonito to southern Jayuya. Multiple rivers in eastern Puerto Rico, mostly those that had been most affected by the previous week���s rain, reached major flooding. Widespread flooding was reported, mostly in southern and central Puerto Rico. Numerous mudslides were also reported throughout the affected areas of steep terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding at the entrance of a residential in Avenida las Americas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wet trend continued by the end of the month as another TUTT approaches the area. At first, this mid to upper level feature combined with moisture being pulling from the deep tropics into the region. Later in the period, a tropical wave was added to the mixture, further increasing moisture content and instability. A Flash Flood Watch was issued due to the excessive rainfall expected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported that road PR-181, near El Verde, was near impassable due to flooding.

Additionally, the soils remained saturated from the rains in September, so several mudslides and flooding reports were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh east-southeast wind flow created choppy seas along the local waters. This triggered moderate rip current risk along the north-facing beaches of Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 29-year-old male apparently was swept away by the rip currents in a beach behind Ashford Hospital in Condado. The individual was in regular clothing and carrying his wallet when found in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of moderate winds and a northeasterly swell resulted in breaking waves of 6 to 11 feet along the northern coast of Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21-year-old male was swept away by a rip current in Playa Los Tubos, Manati. The body was found 3 days laters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and associated shearline, as well as increasing moisture from the Caribbean aided in the development of strong showers and isolated thunderstorms along the central municipalities of Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surge of water and a mudslide broke into a business wall (Boricuas Auto Part) on Road 152.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and associated shearline, as well as increasing moisture from the Caribbean aided in the development of strong showers and isolated thunderstorms along the central municipalities of Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A party of eight people was trapped in Rio Maravilla, Barrio Palmarejo, Corozal, due to a water surge. They were all rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a pre-frontal trough and a shearline increased low-level convergence of moisture along the north-central and northeastern sections of Puerto Rico on the evening of January 31st. This led to the rapid development of thunderstorms and locally heavy showers concentrating over the San Juan metropolitan area and nearby municipalities. Between 2 and 5 inches of rain fell in a period of 2-3 hours, this caused flash flooding across many urban areas of San Juan and Guaynabo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from heavy rainfall affected residences in Torrecillas street in Guaynabo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough combined with local effects generated showers and isolated thunderstorms across the interior and western sections of Puerto Rico on the 1st of February. Some areas received 3 to 4 inches of rainfall, which resulted in flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters from Rio Corozal flooded road 891 next to the local Police Station. No property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a small northerly swell and choppy, wind-driven seas resulted in life-threatening rip currents across most of the north and east-facing beaches of the islands on the 4th of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call alerted the police about a floating person in Flamenco Beach. The person was taken into an ambulance to a hospital, where the doctor confirmed the death by drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and unsettled pattern affected the islands on the first weekend of November. The trough was on the most favorable position for shower development on November 4th, increasing instability aloft. A surface low was induced, causing winds to shift from the south. As a result, a significant flux of moisture was observed. An organized event of rain and thunderstorms was detected, affecting, once again, the south, east and central regions of Puerto Rico. These rains, along with previous rainfalls during the fall months, resulted in numerous reports of mudslides, flooding and rivers overflowing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from broadcast media indicated flooding occurring along a bridge next to La Marqueta El Tamarindo in Adjuntas.

As a result, a significant flux of moisture was observed. An organized event of rain and thunderstorms was detected, affecting, once again, the south, east and central regions of Puerto Rico. These rains, along with previous rainfalls during the fall months, resulted in numerous reports of mudslides, flooding and rivers overflowing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers reported a mudslide near Walgreens in Road PR-385, Penuelas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A TUTT low enhanced the diurnally induced afternoon convection across sections of central and western Puerto Rico between May 23-26. This created long-lasting thunderstorms with periods of heavy rainfall that led to flash flooding and mudslides. Also, the thunderstorms produced small hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MESONET Station UTHP4 in Utuado reported 4.10 inches of rain with the afternoon thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted surface trough, deep tropical moisture, and an upper-level trough led to the development of strong to severe thunderstorms over north-central Puerto Rico during the afternoon hours of May 1st, 2022. Urban flooding and an EF-1 tornado were observed with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the Atlantic coastal waters and over north-central PR merged over Arecibo, and a weak EF-1 tornado formed between Urb. Victor Rojas 2 and PR22 highway. Downed trees, traffic signs, power lines and damage to commercial structures were reported along portions of Ave Industrial and road  PR-129.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported flooding on street 24 in Urb Jardines de Arecibo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers stretching from north to south crossed Puerto Rico during the afternoon hours of March 6th. This resulted in gusty straight-line wind damage across sections of eastern Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree Branches were knocked down by strong wind gusts at Barrio Camarones, Road 836, KM 2.7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong easterly wind flow resulted in hazardous marine and coastal conditions across northern Puerto Rico. This translated in a high rip current risk for many of the local beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A party of three people (two males and a female) were swept away by the rip currents in Playa Poza las Mujeres in Manati. A 25-year-old male drown, while the rest of the party was rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in hazardous seas across the Atlantic waters. Therefore, the risk of rip currents was high for many beaches across Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 66-year-old female tourist was swept away by a rip current and drown in Beach Palmas in Humacao.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong trade winds resulted in hazardous marine and coastal conditions along the northern coast of Puerto Rico, with high rip current risk for much of the local beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 71-year-old male was swept away by the rip currents and drown at Seven Seas in Fajardo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved across the area, increasing convergence at the lower levels. Later in the period, low level moisture was provided by the proximity of a surface trough. Additionally, an upper level low approached from the northeast, providing instability aloft, as high pressure at all level weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter called to report one lane of Road-3 near Home Depot flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Fiona made landfall on Sunday the 18th around 3:20 PM AST near Punta Toc��n in southwestern Puerto Rico.  Strong, gusty winds accompanied much of the stronger convection embedded within the hurricane. Hurricane-force gusts were observed over portions of southern Puerto Rico during the afternoon on Sunday. These gusty winds, combined with ground saturation, resulted in damage across the island due to fallen trees and power lines. ||Fiona resulted in excessive rainfall, especially for southern into eastern Puerto Rico.  In areas along the Cordillera and around El Yunque, widespread rainfall totals of over 16 inches were seen, most of which fell on Sunday. Preliminary estimates suggest that over 24 inches of rain were seen locally, mostly around Cayey and southern Caguas and Aibonito to southern Jayuya. Multiple rivers in eastern Puerto Rico, mostly those that had been most affected by the previous week���s rain, reached major flooding. Widespread flooding was reported, mostly in southern and central Puerto Rico. Numerous mudslides were also reported throughout the affected areas of steep terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lane on the road 109, near the municipal cemetery, collapsed due to excessive rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and unsettled pattern affected the islands on the first weekend of November. The trough was on the most favorable position for shower development on November 4th, increasing instability aloft. A surface low was induced, causing winds to shift from the south. As a result, a significant flux of moisture was observed. An organized event of rain and thunderstorms was detected, affecting, once again, the south, east and central regions of Puerto Rico. These rains, along with previous rainfalls during the fall months, resulted in numerous reports of mudslides, flooding and rivers overflowing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide was reported at road 861 ramal 827, close to the La Loma gas station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The rainfall deficit across east and south of Puerto Rico resulted in drought conditions deteriorating , with Severe Drought (D2) introduced for the northeast, south-central and around Ponce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dry spell caused mandatory water restrictions for the interior and northeast Puerto Rico, including Aibonito, Loiza, Canovanas, Rio Grande, Loiza and Las Piedras. Reservoir levels continued to recede for Carraizo, Cidra, La Plata, Carite, Guajataca, Toa Vaca and Cerrillos. The 28-day average streamflow from USGS river gauge network indicated streamflows running below normal or well below normal across most of Puerto Rico. This drought was of concern to agricultural interests, as hot and dry conditions increased water demands and stressed pastures, crops and native vegetation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation was finally received across Puerto Rico due to a combination of distant Hurricane Earl, a direct hit from Hurricane Fiona and a series of mid to upper-level troughs. As a result, the drought conditions abruptly ended in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The precursor of Hurricane Earl brought above normal rainfalls for the eastern half of Puerto Rico during the first few days of the month. These rains resulted in drought improvement below Severe Drought (D2) criteria. By the mid to latter part of the month, additional rainfalls, mainly due to Hurricane Fiona, resulted in the removal of all drought categories across the territory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation was finally received across Puerto Rico due to a combination of distant Hurricane Earl, a direct hit from Hurricane Fiona and a series of mid to upper-level troughs. As a result, the drought conditions abruptly ended in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The precursor of Hurricane Earl brought above normal rainfalls for the eastern half of Puerto Rico during the first few days of the month. These rains resulted in drought improvement below Severe Drought (D2) criteria. By the mid to latter part of the month, additional rainfalls, mainly due to Hurricane Fiona, resulted in the removal of all drought categories across the territory.
